# Chapter 5: The Wrath of Mars

**Applying Guy Sclanders' Methodology**

## Session Overview

Mars arrives. The god of war has been watching every choice the party made across five sessions. Now he demands satisfaction — not as a villain, but as a force of nature that operates on divine logic. The final session is a reckoning: with a god, with Rome, and with who these characters have become.

**Level:** 7 (final level)
**Duration:** 3–4 hours
**Key Goal:** Survive Mars' judgment; determine the shape of the ending
**Theme:** All five themes converge: Duty, Ambition, Fate, Loyalty, Divine Intervention

### Guy Sclanders' Principles Applied

**All Threads Resolve:** Every major NPC choice, every piece of evidence gathered, every corruption level, every sacrifice — it all feeds into this session. The ending the party gets is the one they built.

**Meaningful Sacrifice:** Mars does not ask for nothing. The question is what each character is willing to give, and what giving it means.

**Player Agency Through Consequences:** There is no "correct" ending. Each outcome is a direct product of what the party chose to value across five sessions.

---

## Pre-Session Preparation

### Continuity Tracker

Run through this before any prep. The session changes based on everything that came before.

| Session | Choice | Session 5 effect |
|---|---|---|
| S1 | Spear given to Legate | Mars is angrier — the spear was used for mortal ambition |
| S1 | Spear destroyed | Mars is angrier — his property was destroyed without his permission |
| S1 | Spear retained by party | Mars respects the caution; the trial is slightly less severe |
| S2 | Vercingetorix allied | He sends a message: "Face the god honestly. He respects honesty." |
| S2 | Tribune alive and cooperative | His testimony helped stop Brutus — Mars notes that duty was served |
| S3 | Sacrifice at the grove was real | Mars acknowledges the offering; reduces his opening wrath |
| S3 | Sacrifice was token | Mars is contemptuous; harder initial checks |
| S3 | Someone at corruption 5 | They are present in the arena — but not as themselves. Mars uses them. |
| S4 | Emperor saved | Mars is marginally impressed (protecting a warrior-emperor has value) |
| S4 | Brutus stopped permanently | Mars notes this — ambition checked by duty |
| S4 | Brutus escaped | Mars considers this unfinished business; longer epilogue required |

### Props & Handouts

**Handout 10: Mars' Brand (give to each player at Session 5 end)**
```
[A small card per player, hand-drawn or printed]

THE MARK OF MARS

[Draw a stylized spear and wolf — Mars' symbols]

This character bears the god's attention. They have stood
before a god of war and lived. For the rest of their days:

  • Advantage on Intimidation checks
  • Other soldiers recognize something in them they cannot name
  • Once per long rest: ask the DM "What does Mars want
    from this situation?" and receive an honest answer.

In exchange: Mars may call on them. Once. At his choosing.
```

**Physical Props:**
- A black d20 set aside — "Mars' die." Roll it openly for his attack rolls. Let the players see.
- Prepare the arena weapons as index cards (see Scene 2)

**Handout 11: Arena Weapon Cards**
*Print or write each on a separate card, place them face-down on the table.*
```
GLADIUS OF ACHILLES
Taken from a hero who chose glory over survival.
+2 weapon. Advantage on attack rolls.
Vulnerability to piercing damage (the price of arrogance).

SHIELD OF HORATIUS
Carried at the Tiber bridge by a man who held alone.
+3 AC. Reaction: impose disadvantage on one attack per round.
Cannot be used with a two-handed weapon.

SPEAR OF LEONIDAS
Three hundred held. None survived. The spear remembers.
Reach 10 ft. +1d10 damage if you haven't moved this turn.
At 0 HP: you stabilize automatically once per combat.
```

---

## Scene 0: Cold Open (5 minutes)

**Three days after the Triumph.** Whatever the outcome in Rome, the city is still buzzing — the aftermath of either the foiled assassination or its partial success, celebrations or investigations in the Senate.

**Read aloud (slow, deliberate):**

> *Noon.*
>
> *The sky turns red.*
>
> *Not the red of sunset — that slow warm bleeding at the horizon. This is blood red, from zenith to rim, everywhere at once, as if someone poured a cup of Mars' wine across the entire dome of heaven.*
>
> *The Tiber, in full view from the city's hills, reverses direction. You can see it from where you stand. The current simply... turns.*
>
> *A statue of Mars in the Forum — marble, twelve feet tall, serene for two hundred years — begins to weep. The tears run red down its face and pool in the Forum's stones.*
>
> *Every dog in Rome howls. All of them. At once. Then silence.*
>
> *The augurs don't need to deliberate.*
>
> *That night, you all dream the same dream.*

**The Dream:**

> *A vast arena. Black sand. A sky the color of a wound.*
>
> *On a throne built from ten thousand weapons — swords, spears, shields, all the iron humanity has ever bent toward violence — sits a figure in armor that doesn't reflect light so much as absorb it.*
>
> *When he speaks, the sound isn't sound. It moves through the ground, through your bones:*
>
> **"YOU. MORTALS WHO MADE THEMSELVES MY CONCERN. I HAVE WATCHED YOU ACROSS FIVE SEASONS OF DEATH AND CHOICE. NOW YOU ANSWER TO ME. COME."**
>
> *You wake in the arena.*

---

## Scene 1: The Arena

The party materialises standing on the black sand. No transition, no blur — they were asleep and now they are here.

**The Arena:**
- Circular, 200 feet across
- Black sand that absorbs sound (no echoes)
- Walls lined with the weapons of fallen heroes — ancient, from civilizations Rome never knew
- The arena weapons (Handout 11 cards) are visible on the walls — the party can see them from where they stand
- No exits except through Mars' judgment

**Mars' Presence:**

He doesn't rush. He sits. He looks at them the way a general looks at a battlefield — assessing, calculating. When he finally speaks, his voice is not loud. It fills the space because the space has no room for anything else.

> *"I know every choice you made. I know the ones you made in the dark, when no one was watching. I know the ones you almost made.*
>
> *"The spear is gone. My property. My son's legacy. Gone because you decided it was yours to unmake.*
>
> *"So. Here is what I offer: prove to me it was worth it. Prove that the mortals who destroyed a god's weapon had the right."*

**Mars' Challenge:**

He lays out options without preference. He is genuinely testing, not performing.

> *"One of you, in single combat against a champion of my choosing. Or all of you, against me. Or" — a pause, something that might be interest — "you speak. Tell me why you did what you did. Make me believe you."*

**Option A: Single Champion** — Mars summons a Divine Champion
**Option B: Full party vs. Mars himself**
**Option C: Argument — the hardest option that costs the least blood**

---

## Scene 2: The Trial

### Option A — Champion Combat

Mars summons a **Divine Champion**: a perfect warrior drawn from the Pool of the Heroic Dead. Not a monster — a person, with a face, who fights with complete discipline.

> *From the sand rises a soldier. Roman armor. But the armor is too old — pre-Republic Roman, iron-age craftsmanship. The face is calm.*
>
> *Mars: "Petronius. The first man to hold a bridge over the Tiber. He died so Rome could survive. Let's see if you deserve what he bought."*

**Divine Champion:**
- HP: 140 | AC: 18 | Speed: 30 ft
- **Multiattack:** Three attacks with spear (+9 to hit, 1d8+5 piercing + 1d8 radiant)
- **Divine Resilience:** Resistance to all non-radiant, non-magical damage
- **War God's Patience:** If the champion is reduced to 50 HP or less, Mars calls "Enough" — the champion stands down regardless. Endurance matters as much as winning.
- **Legendary: Repost (1/round):** When missed with a melee attack, makes one attack as a reaction

**Arena Weapons** (flip the Handout 11 cards face-up): Any character can retrieve an arena weapon as a **bonus action** on their turn. Each weapon has a cost. Make sure both the power and the cost are clear before they choose.

**Victory Condition:** Reduce the champion to 0 HP — **OR** survive 10 rounds. Mars respects endurance as much as victory.

---

### Option B — Full Party vs. Mars

This is the harder path. Mars does not hold back, but he is also genuinely testing — he won't end them quickly. He wants to see what they're made of.

**Mars, God of War:**
- HP: 300 | AC: 20 | Speed: 40 ft
- **Legendary Resistance (3/day):** If he fails a saving throw, he succeeds instead
- **Multiattack:** Four attacks with the God-Forged Blade (+12 to hit, 2d8+7 slashing + 1d8 fire)
- **Divine Smite (Recharge 5-6):** 30-foot radius, DC 18 Con save, 8d8 fire damage (half on success)
- **War Cry (Recharge 4-6):** DC 16 Wisdom save, Frightened condition until end of next turn

**Legendary Actions (3/turn, end of other characters' turns):**
- **Strike (1 action):** One weapon attack
- **Summon Fallen (2 actions):** Four Shadows of the Heroic Dead appear (use Specter stats, CR 1 each)
- **God's Eye (1 action):** Mars sees through all illusion and invisibility until his next turn

**The Tactical Reality:** Mars at full power is beyond what a level 7 party can defeat through damage alone. The arena weapons help significantly. And Mars has a weakness: he respects *tactics* used against him. A party that uses clever positioning, the arena features, or unexpected approaches (including talking mid-combat) can gain advantage on attack rolls for one round as Mars pauses to appreciate the play.

**Victory Condition:** Reduce Mars to 150 HP. At that point he stops — not defeated, but satisfied. *"Enough. You fight as well as I hoped."*

---

### Option C — The Argument

The hardest option in terms of nerve. But potentially the most powerful ending.

A character steps forward and speaks directly to Mars. No weapons. No posturing.

**What Mars actually wants to hear** (not told to the players, but what moves him):

The key is understanding what Mars values. He does not value peace. He does not value mercy as a principle. He does not care about politics. He cares about: honor, courage, discipline, and the acknowledgment that war has a purpose beyond destruction. He despises waste -- the idea that people died for ambition instead of for something real.

The three arguments that genuinely move him:

1. *"The spear was dishonoring your name."* The corruption it caused was not war -- it was rot. It turned men against their tent-brothers, made soldiers murder each other in the dark. That is not what Mars stands for. Destroying it was an act of respect, not defiance. **Evidence that helps:** describing the corrupted worker, the influenced NPC's suffering, Brutus' plan to use divine power for an assassination.

2. *"We destroyed it not to defy you but because we understood what you actually represent."* Mars is the god of disciplined, honorable conflict. The spear had become a tool of ignoble violence. Destroying it was protecting his domain. **Evidence that helps:** the sacrifice at the grove (especially if it was genuine), any moment where the party chose duty over survival.

3. *"We know the difference between war and murder."* This is the most specific and the most powerful. It requires the speaker to name an act of the campaign where the party made a harder, more honorable choice when an easier, crueler one was available. **Evidence that helps:** treating Vercingetorix with respect, protecting the legionaries at the river crossing rather than killing them, attempting to capture Marius Coda rather than simply cutting him down.

**Arguments that anger him:**

- *"The spear was too dangerous for anyone to have."* This is not a moral argument; it is a practical one. Mars does not care about practicality. He cares about principle.
- *"We had no choice."* You always have a choice. Mortals who tell Mars they had no choice are telling him they're not worth talking to.
- *"We were trying to protect Rome."* Mars is not a Roman god. He is older than Rome. He does not care what Rome's interests are.

**The Roll:**

- DC 19 Persuasion -- or DC 17 if the party invokes specific evidence from the above (the corruption it caused, Brutus' plan, the possessed NPC's suffering)
- Add the sacrifice made at the grove: if it was genuine, advantage on this roll
- Add Vercingetorix's message (if alive and allied): he provided the exact words that move Mars -- using them gives a +3 bonus
- **Critical failure (roll 1-5):** Mars laughs. Not cruel laughter -- contemptuous. *"WORDS. Mortals and their words."* Combat begins. Disadvantage on all rolls in round 1 because the party has burned the moment of stillness.
- **Failure (6-16):** *"Try harder."* One more chance at DC 14, but now they must demonstrate rather than speak. Show a scar. Invoke the name of someone who died. Describe exactly what the spear's corruption looked like, specifically, in a person's face. Mars is watching for whether they flinch.
- **Success (17+):** Mars is quiet for a long moment. Then: *"...You are not wrong."* The silence before those words is the most important moment in the session. Let it sit.
- **Critical success (natural 20):** *"...You are not wrong. And you knew you weren't wrong before you came here, which is rarer."* The Chosen mechanic is offered to two characters instead of one.

---

## Post-Trial Narration

After Option A or Option B concludes, before Mars rises, give the table a moment of stillness. This read-aloud is for the transition:

**After Option A (champion combat):**

> *The Divine Champion does not fall dramatically. He simply stops. His weapon lowers. He looks at you -- really looks at you, not with the assessing calm of combat but with something older, something like recognition.*
>
> *Then he is gone. Not dissolved, not defeated -- simply elsewhere, back to wherever heroes wait between moments of use.*
>
> *The arena is quiet. The black sand settles. Your breath comes hard.*
>
> *From the throne, a sound: not quite approval, not quite satisfaction, but something in the register of a man watching a thing go better than he expected.*
>
> *Mars stands.*

**After Option B (full party vs. Mars):**

> *At 150 HP remaining, he stops mid-swing.*
>
> *He looks at the weapon in his hand. Then at you. Then back at the weapon.*
>
> *The pause is five seconds. At the table of a god, five seconds is long enough to mean something.*
>
> *"Enough," he says. Not as a command. As a verdict.*
>
> *The God-Forged Blade disappears. He reaches up and removes the helmet -- not a gesture you expected, not from him -- and for a moment he simply looks like a very large, very tired being who has been doing this since before your civilization had a word for war.*
>
> *"Enough," he says again, and this time it sounds like he means something more than the combat.*
>
> *He stands.*

---

## Scene 3: The Price of Standing Before a God

**After combat or after the argument succeeds:**

Mars rises from his throne. Up close he is not monstrous -- he is *immense*. Not just in height but in presence, in weight, in the sense that he has existed since the first time a person picked up a rock and raised it in anger.

**Read aloud -- Mars up close:**

> *He stands. He is taller than you would have guessed from a distance. The armor he wears does not reflect light: it absorbs it, the way old iron absorbs heat, slowly and completely.*
>
> *His face is not a god's face in the way you imagined. It is a general's face. Lined. Watchful. The face of someone who has been in the room when decisions are made and has never once pretended those decisions are clean.*
>
> *He looks at each of you in turn. Not with warmth and not with hostility. With the specific attentiveness of someone who is deciding how to remember you.*
>
> *When he speaks, his voice is quieter than it was from the throne. It is still not quite a sound. It moves through the arena floor, through your bones, but gently. The way a bell sounds after you've walked out of the room where it rang.*

He looks at each of them individually. Then:

> *"You have proven yourselves. Few mortals have stood in this arena and left standing. Fewer still have faced a god and spoken honestly.*
>
> *"I am not appeased. I will not lie to you — my weapon is gone and I am not appeased. But I am..." — the word seems unfamiliar in his mouth — "...satisfied. You have the quality I value. The others who touched that spear did not.*
>
> *"So. You live. But you leave here changed. Everything leaves this arena changed."*

### The Consequences — Choose 2 or 3 Based on Campaign Arc

Lay these out honestly. Each is a real thing that will follow these characters for the rest of their lives.

**The Mark of War** *(Handout 10 — give to all players)*
Every character bears Mars' brand — a faint red scar that glows in moonlight or when violence is imminent. They have advantage on Intimidation checks. Other soldiers recognize something in them they cannot name and give ground. Once per long rest: they may ask the DM "What does Mars want here?" and receive an honest answer.
*In exchange: Mars may call on them once. At his choosing.*

**The Chosen** *(one character, by group vote or natural story fit)*
One character becomes Mars' Chosen — not a servant, but a champion. They gain: resistance to fire and poison, one extra attack per turn on their first attack action. They must: answer one call to war per year, somewhere, for someone who has no army. Mars will tell them when and where. They don't get to choose the cause — only whether to answer.

**The Debt** *(for a party that bargained rather than fought)*
Mars accepted their argument. He does not give things for free. He will call in one favor at a time of his choosing — not within the next year, but sometime. The nature of the favor is unspecified. He will not ask them to do something dishonorable. He will ask them to do something hard.

**The Transformation** *(only if a player chooses this)*
A character offers to stay. To become a guardian of Mars' arena — not enslaved, but devoted. They leave the mortal world. They gain something that cannot be quantified in game terms: a place at the table of divine affairs, and the knowledge that they ended their story on their own terms.
This is a noble sacrifice ending. The player narrates their last moment.

**The Prophecy** *(for groups that want a sequel hook)*
> *"One last thing. In three generations, this city will burn from within. Not by barbarian hands — from its own weight, its own rot. The seeds are already planted. I tell you this not because I want it stopped. I tell you because I want to see if your children's children have the same quality you do."*

---

## Scene 4: Return to Rome

The party wakes in the Temple of Mars in Rome. Dawn light through the columns. They're lying on the stone floor, each bearing the brand, each carrying the weight of what happened.

The temple is empty except for one priest — an old man who has been tending this temple for forty years. He looks at the brands on their arms and says nothing for a long moment.

Then: *"I've been waiting for something like this. I didn't know what I was waiting for."*

He gives them water. That's all.

---

## Scene 5: Epilogue — Where Are They Now?

Go around the table. Each player answers for their character: one year later.

**DM prompts:**
- Where are you?
- What are you doing?
- Has Mars' mark changed how people treat you?
- What do you remember most clearly from this campaign?
- What do you dream about?

**Then:** Each player asks the DM about one other character -- an NPC they cared about. The DM answers honestly.

Use these as prompts: the DM describes the NPC's current state, then invites the player to ask one follow-up question, which the DM answers in character (as if the NPC is speaking directly).

| NPC | One-year-later state | Sample follow-up answer |
|---|---|---|
| **Legate Corvinus** | Disgraced or dead, depending on Session 2. If alive: civilian, Campania, farming grapes, drinking too much of them. The man who had a god's weapon pass through his hands and couldn't hold it. | If asked what he regrets: *"I thought I was playing a game I understood. I was not."* |
| **Augur Cassia** | Left the legion's service. She runs a small shrine on the Danube frontier, training augurs who wash out of the official colleges -- the ones who see too much to ignore. She has stopped trying to explain what she is. | If asked whether the omens have gotten quieter: *"No. But I have gotten better at knowing which ones matter."* |
| **Centurion Titus Varro** | Retired. Has the farmland in Umbria he mentioned once and never again. Writes occasional letters to the party. They're always short. The last one said: *"Harvest good. No one tried to kill me this month. Recommend it."* | If asked whether he thinks about the campaign: *"Every morning. It's why I water the vines myself. Something to do with my hands that doesn't end in blood."* |
| **Tribune Lucius** | In prison (if betrayed by his own Senate testimony), exonerated (if Brutus' fall protected him), or quietly fled to his family estates in Sicily and stays there. If free: changed. Quieter. Less ring-wearing. | If asked what changed him: *"Finding out my father was wrong. I'd spent my entire life assuming he was right. The architecture of that assumption takes a while to clear."* |
| **Vercingetorix** | Dead within the year -- his illness progressed as he said it would. His funeral pyre was visible from the Roman watchtowers on the Limes. His people sent the party a brief message: two Elder Futhark runes scratched on a piece of birchbark. TIWAZ and WUNJO -- war and joy. | If asked what the runes mean together: *"He said, at the end, that a good death was a joy. He got one."* (This is from Thusnelda, who sends a separate note.) |
| **Senator Brutus** | Dead (executed), in exile (stripped of property and citizenship), or at large (fled east with resources and a plan). If at large: a rumor surfaces from Alexandria -- a man matching his description has been seen at the temple of Bellona, meeting with men who wear the symbols of Mars. | If asked what he would say to the party if they met again: *"Something like: 'You were right and I was not wrong.' He would mean both halves."* |
| **Thusnelda** | At the grove. The iron fragments are all buried. She is training a successor -- a young Germanic woman the party may have glimpsed during the ritual. She does not expect to live more than five years. She is at peace about this. | If asked whether the grove is different now: *"It is quieter. The wound is closed. Quiet is what you get when something has finished being in pain."* |

**Final DM narration:**

> *The shadow of Mars is long. What you did in those five sessions echoes across the Empire — in the Senate, in the barracks, in a Germanic forest that is slowly reclaiming the ground where a sacred grove once stood.*
>
> *Some call you heroes. Some call you fools. Some call you god-marked and cross the street to avoid you.*
>
> *Mars himself calls you interesting. In the divine realm, that is far more dangerous than being forgotten.*
>
> *Your story is over. Legends never are.*

---

## DM Notes

### OGAS This Session

**Mars, God of War**
- **Objective:** Reclaim his honor and understand what these mortals are
- **Goal (this session):** Test them — genuinely, not as performance
- **Agenda:** Present options; respond authentically to whatever they choose; be harder on them if the grove sacrifice was token
- **Secret:** He was going to let the spear remain corrupted and eventually destroy whoever held it — he'd written it off. These mortals forced him to pay attention to something he'd given up on. He's grateful. He will never say this.

### Corruption Level 5 in the Arena

A character at corruption level 5 enters the arena no longer entirely themselves. The spear's influence has taken root deep enough that Mars can perceive the connection. He uses this.

**In Option A (champion combat):** The level-5 character does not fight with the party. At the start of the combat, Mars gestures at them and says: *"That one stays still."* The character is held -- not magically paralyzed, but the divine command is a weight they must push against. DC 16 Wisdom save at the start of each turn to act freely; on a failure they can only take the Dodge action. Mars is testing whether the corruption has consumed them completely, or whether they can push back. If they succeed on 3 saving throws across the combat: the connection weakens. Their corruption reduces to 4 at the end of the session.

**In Option B (full party vs. Mars):** The level-5 character gains a different problem: Mars' attacks target them with advantage. Not out of cruelty -- he is pulling at the corruption, seeing if he can use them as leverage. Mid-combat, he speaks to them directly: *"You wanted what the spear offered. Part of you still does. That part is mine to use."* This is the moment for that character to choose: fight their own corruption on top of the combat, or acknowledge it aloud to the party. If they acknowledge it: the party can use the "called to them three times" mechanic from Session 3 to break the connection mid-combat, giving that character immunity to Mars' targeting for the rest of the fight.

**In Option C (the argument):** The level-5 character is the voice Mars uses to test the speaker. At the moment of the DC roll, Mars says: *"Before you answer -- your companion. What do they want?"* and turns his full attention to the corrupted character. If that character says what the corruption wants (the spear's power, glory, victory), the Persuasion check gains a -5 penalty. If that character says what they themselves actually want -- which might be the same thing, or might not -- Mars accepts the answer and the check proceeds normally. This is the corrupted character's redemption moment.

**After the trial:** A level-5 character cannot have their corruption reduced by the Mark of War alone. They need to explicitly choose The Transformation or accept The Debt, with the debt being specifically: they must do something selfless, at personal cost, within the next year, witnessed by no one important. Mars will know.

### The Call to War: Practical Mechanics for The Chosen

The Chosen receives Mars' brand and, eventually, a call. This is meant to be a future-game hook, but the DM should give the player enough information to know what it means.

**How Mars calls:** The Chosen begins to dream of a specific location -- not a vision with context, but a pull, like the spear's pull but without the corruption. They know a direction and, over several days, increasingly specific details: a terrain type, a smell, weather. It resolves into a location within a week.

**When:** Mars calls once. Not within the first year -- he respects their need to recover. Sometime between one and five years after the campaign. The DM should decide this based on what story feels right.

| Type of call | What Mars needs | What refusing costs |
|---|---|---|
| **The unwinnable battle** | The Chosen to stand and hold while others escape; they will survive but barely | The location is lost; a community is destroyed; Mars' attention moves to someone less worthy |
| **The judgment** | The Chosen to arbitrate a conflict between two parties who will accept no other arbiter | If refused: war breaks out. Mars marks it in his accounting. |
| **The escort** | Protect one person on a journey through territory where the Chosen is the only one who can keep them alive | If refused: the person dies. Mars notes that the Chosen chose comfort over duty. |
| **The last stand** | This one is simple: Mars needs a warrior in a specific place, doing a specific thing, knowing they will not come back | This call is never made lightly. Mars asks it only of a Chosen who spent their time since the campaign living up to what the arena showed him. |

The Chosen does not have to accept. Mars does not punish refusal with divine retribution. He simply notes it. And the next time someone asks "What does Mars want from this situation?" -- the honest answer is more complicated.

### Reading the Party's Ending

The right ending is the one that matches what the party built across five sessions. Guide the epilogue toward the tone that fits:

| Party arc | Tone of ending |
|---|---|
| Loyal Romans, saved the Emperor, cooperated with Senate | **Heroic** — public honors, genuine sense of duty fulfilled |
| Pragmatists who made hard choices, lost people, finished the job | **Earned** — quiet satisfaction, no parade, they know what it cost |
| Lost party members, sacrificed much, barely survived | **Bittersweet** — they saved something but it took everything |
| Defied Rome at every turn, went their own way | **Legendary** — they're outside the system now; marked by a god; free and untethered |

### Closing the Campaign

After the epilogue: thank the players. Name one thing each character did that made the campaign better. Ask what surprised them. Ask what they'll remember.

This is part of the session. It matters.

**Sequel Hooks** *(if desired)*
- Mars' prophecy: three generations, Rome burns from within
- Brutus' cult, if he escaped: growing in Alexandria and Antioch
- Other gods took notice. Minerva in particular found the argument the party made to Mars interesting.
- Vercingetorix's tribe: leaderless, but they remember the Romans who helped destroy the God-Killer. Something might grow from that.
- The iron fragments of the spear, buried in seven places by Thusnelda. Six of them are still there.

### Guy Sclanders' Principles: Final Accounting

**OGAS:** Every NPC's Objective, Goal, Agenda, and Secret resolved across the campaign in a way consistent with their character.

**Strong Openings:** All five sessions began in media res, with a crisis already in motion.

**Pressure Cooker:** Each session escalated the stakes. Personal → Fort → Regional → Imperial → Divine.

**Meaningful Choices:** No session had a "right" answer. Every decision fed the next one.

**Three Clue Rule:** Every mystery had three independent paths. The party didn't need all three.

**Sacrifice Principle:** From the grove ritual to the arena's price, victory cost something real.

**Reactive World:** Every NPC pursued their OGAS whether or not the party engaged them. Varro armed his men. Cassia traveled to Rome. Brutus built toward the Triumph. Vercingetorix watched the fort for weeks. The world moved.

**Player Agency Through Consequences:** Multiple valid endings. No single correct path. The campaign ends with the ending they built.
